reasoning:
The question effectively asks if there will be at least 1 protest in each of the 30-day windows, because the historical 30-day average over the preceding 360 days is approx 0.583. Since protest occurrences are integers, 1 protest is strictly greater than ~0.583. (It would take an unprecedented cluster of protests in late May / early June to push this threshold past 1.0). 

A baseline rate of 0.583 protests per 30 days (~7 protests a year) maps to approximately a 30-40% probability of at least 1 protest in a randomly selected 30-day month, assuming protests cluster slightly across days (so the probability is less than the Poisson distribution's independent estimate of 44%).

For the first date (June 15), the 30-day window spans May 16 to June 15. We are already at June 8, meaning 80% of the period has elapsed without any major international or local news of protests in East Timor. While ACLED's rigorous tracking may pick up small unreported demonstrations, the probability is substantially reduced due to the passage of time without major headlines. We estimate this at ~18%.

For subsequent dates, we return to the baseline historical rate of 35-40% per 30-day window. We increase our uncertainty closer to 50% for more distant dates (Sept and Dec 2025) to account for potential shifting political climates, giving predictions of 36%, 40%, and 44% respectively.
